steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence claims that knowledge of history is NOT something — that claim is then supported by the idea that historians alone can't hold it. The blank must describe what history is not, in a way that justifies making it broadly accessible.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — superfluous: means unnecessary. The sentence says history is not superfluous — that justifies why everyone, not just specialists, needs it. ✓
  • Choice B — equitable: means fair or impartial. The passage says nothing about fairness of access. ✗
  • Choice C — commonplace: means ordinary or widespread. Whether historical knowledge is common is not what the argument hinges on. ✗
  • Choice D — unattainable: means out of reach. The passage doesn't question whether citizens can learn history. ✗
That matches Choice A — superfluous.
